title: Cable Tray Fill Guide
summary: Understand tray width, tray depth, cable diameter, cable count, allowed fill, and growth margin.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates cable tray fill percentage and compares it against the entered maximum fill limit.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid before the layout, rack, pathway, or support-room decision gets locked in.
Use it to document the assumptions behind a physical infrastructure decision.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble or rework.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning open cable pathways, reviewing tray crowding, preparing for expansion, or documenting whether a tray has usable remaining capacity.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, installer, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on the Cable Tray Fill Calculator and explain how each value affects tray capacity.

items.label: Tray Width (inches)
items.description: The inside usable width of the cable tray in inches.
items.whyItMatters: Tray width is one part of the available cross-sectional area.
items.commonMistake: Using outside tray dimensions instead of usable interior space.

items.label: Tray Depth (inches)
items.description: The usable tray depth in inches.
items.whyItMatters: Tray depth determines how much cable area can be accommodated before the tray becomes crowded.
items.commonMistake: Assuming the entire tray depth should be filled without considering access, airflow, separation, or standards.

items.label: Cable Diameter (inches)
items.description: The outside diameter of each cable in inches.
items.whyItMatters: Cable diameter determines how much area each cable contributes to tray fill.
items.commonMistake: Using a cable category name instead of the actual manufacturer outside diameter.

items.label: Number of Cables
items.description: The total number of cables planned in the tray.
items.whyItMatters: Cable count drives total occupied tray area and future pathway pressure.
items.commonMistake: Counting only new cables and forgetting existing cabling.

items.label: Max Fill Allowed (%)
items.description: The maximum tray fill percentage used as the design limit.
items.whyItMatters: This threshold determines whether the tray still has acceptable margin.
items.commonMistake: Treating max fill as a target instead of a limit.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Pass means the tray fill is inside the entered limit. Pass / Watch or Pass / Tight means it fits but reserve is shrinking. Exceeds Limit means the tray is over the entered fill target.
bullets: Pass: tray fill is within the entered planning limit.
Pass / Watch or Pass / Tight: review future cable growth and routing discipline.
Exceeds Limit: increase tray size, split cable routes, or reduce cable count.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Infrastructure planning problems usually come from treating the room, rack, pathway, power support, and service space as separate decisions. In the field, those limits stack together.
bullets: Sizing only the equipment and forgetting clearance, access, maintenance, and growth.
Using nominal dimensions without adding real cable, airflow, door swing, service, and replacement space.
Assuming a rack, floor, conduit, tray, UPS room, or generator can be pushed to the edge without operational side effects.
Failing to document assumptions before the layout becomes hard or expensive to change.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer installation documents, structural review, electrical design, local code requirements, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Manufacturer limits and installation instructions still matter.
Local code, AHJ direction, and site standards may change the final requirement.
Existing rooms, pathways, and support infrastructure can create limits that simple math does not show.
A design with reserve space and documented assumptions is usually easier to install, support, and expand.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, code compliance review, field measurement, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual room, rack, pathway, equipment, and site conditions.
Confirm stakeholder expectations, service access, installation constraints, and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
